name: Imsety
description: Imsety, also known as Amset, is one of the four sons of Horus in Egyptian mythology, specifically associated with the protection of the deceased. He is a funerary deity responsible for guarding the liver of the deceased, which was removed during mummification and placed in a canopic jar. Imsety is typically depicted with a human head, distinguishing him from his brothers who have animal heads. He is under the protection of the goddess Isis, who watches over him and ensures the safety of the organ he guards. Imsety's role is crucial in the afterlife, as preserving the organs was believed to be essential for the deceased's resurrection and journey into the next world. He represents the southern direction and is often associated with warmth and vitality. His canopic jar was typically made of calcite and placed near the sarcophagus, demonstrating the importance of his role in the funerary rites. Imsety's presence in the tomb ensured the deceased would have the necessary components for a successful afterlife, highlighting his significance in the Egyptian belief system. He is a vital figure in ensuring the completeness and well-being of the deceased in the afterlife. The meticulous preservation of the liver under Imsety's care symbolized the Egyptians' deep respect for the body and its essential functions, even after death, paving the way for eternal life.
subtitle: Guardian of the Liver, Son of Horus

keyMyths.title: The Sons of Horus
keyMyths.description: Imsety, along with his brothers Hapi, Duamutef, and Qebehsenuef, were tasked with protecting the internal organs of the deceased during mummification. Each son guarded a specific organ and was associated with a cardinal direction. Imsety specifically guarded the liver.

keyMyths.title: Protection by Isis
keyMyths.description: Isis, as the protector of Imsety, ensured his safety and effectiveness in guarding the liver. Her magical powers and protective spells were crucial in ensuring the deceased's journey to the afterlife. This divine protection was essential for the success of the mummification process.

keyMyths.title: The Canopic Jars
keyMyths.description: The canopic jars, each holding an organ and guarded by one of the four sons of Horus, were essential components of the funerary process. Imsety's jar, often made of calcite and shaped with a human head, symbolized his role and provided a safe vessel for the liver.

sources.author: E.A. Wallis Budge
